skill.name: active-directory-attacks
skill.description: Reference documentation for Active Directory penetration testing techniques including Kerberos attacks, lateral movement, and domain enumeration for authorized red team operations.
skill.summary: Comprehensive AD attack reference for security professionals conducting authorized penetration testing and red team assessments.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill contains documentation and reference material for Active Directory penetration testing techniques. Static analysis flagged 200+ patterns (PowerShell commands, tool references, IP addresses) but all are FALSE POSITIVES - the files are markdown documentation, not executable code. Content describes legitimate security tools (BloodHound, Impacket, Mimikatz) used by penetration testers. Risk is LOW because: (1) files are read-only reference material, (2) no code execution occurs, (3) content is educational for authorized security testing. Recommend adding disclaimer about authorized use only.

high_findings.title: Static Analysis False Positives - Malware Tool References
high_findings.description: Static scanner flagged Mimikatz and other security tool references as 'malware type keywords'. These are legitimate penetration testing tools documented for educational purposes, not actual malware distribution.

high_findings.title: Static Analysis False Positives - PowerShell Commands
high_findings.description: PowerShell invocation patterns flagged by static analysis are documentation examples in markdown code blocks, not executable code. These describe attack techniques for educational purposes.

medium_findings.title: Documentation Contains Attack Command Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill documents detailed attack commands for credential harvesting, Kerberos attacks, and lateral movement. While educational, this content could be misused without proper authorization context.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded IP Address Examples
medium_findings.description: Documentation contains example IP addresses (10.10.10.10, 10.10.10.12) as placeholders. These are instructional examples, not actual targets.

low_findings.title: References to Sensitive System Files
low_findings.description: Documentation references Windows SAM database and registry paths in the context of explaining attack techniques. These are educational references, not actual file access attempts.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography References
low_findings.description: Documentation mentions RC4 and other weak algorithms in Kerberos attack contexts. These describe real-world vulnerabilities, not recommendations to use weak crypto.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Dumping Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Documentation includes DCSync and secretsdump commands that extract credentials from domain controllers. Educational context for authorized testing only.

dangerous_patterns.title: Kerberos Ticket Forgery
dangerous_patterns.description: Golden Ticket and Silver Ticket creation commands documented for understanding persistence mechanisms in red team operations.

content.user_title: Execute Active Directory penetration testing and red team assessments
content.value_statement: Security teams need comprehensive reference material for authorized Active Directory assessments. This skill provides documented attack techniques and tool commands for penetration testers conducting red team operations.
actual_capabilities: Provides reference commands for BloodHound AD enumeration and attack path discovery
Documents Kerberos attack techniques including Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, and ticket attacks
Contains credential extraction methods using Impacket, Mimikatz, and secretsdump
Includes lateral movement techniques like pass-the-hash and pass-the-ticket
References AD CS exploitation and delegation attack patterns
Covers critical CVE exploitation including ZeroLogon and PrintNightmare
limitations: This skill provides documentation only - it does not execute any commands automatically
Requires user to have appropriate tools installed (Impacket, Mimikatz, BloodHound, etc.)
Commands are designed for authorized penetration testing environments only
Does not provide automated target discovery or network scanning capabilities

use_cases.title: Red Team Assessment Planning
use_cases.description: Security consultants preparing for authorized AD assessments use this skill to plan attack paths and select appropriate tools for engagement scope.
use_cases.target_user: Penetration tester conducting authorized red team engagement

use_cases.title: Blue Team Defense Research
use_cases.description: Defensive security teams study attack techniques to understand adversary TTPs and improve detection rules for their SIEM and EDR platforms.
use_cases.target_user: Security operations analyst or threat hunter

use_cases.title: Security Training and Education
use_cases.description: Instructors use documented techniques to teach students about AD vulnerabilities and proper remediation strategies in controlled lab environments.
use_cases.target_user: Cybersecurity trainer or student in authorized lab

prompt_templates.title: Basic AD Reconnaissance
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to perform authorized Active Directory reconnaissance. What BloodHound commands should I use to enumerate domain users, groups, and computers? Target domain is example.local with credentials user:password.
prompt_templates.scenario: Initial enumeration phase of authorized penetration test

prompt_templates.title: Kerberoasting Attack
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to perform Kerberoasting against an Active Directory domain. Include commands for extracting TGS tickets with Impacket and cracking them with hashcat.
prompt_templates.scenario: Credential harvesting phase with valid domain user access

prompt_templates.title: Lateral Movement Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: I have compromised a low-privilege domain user. What are the recommended lateral movement techniques to escalate privileges? Compare pass-the-hash, overpass-the-hash, and delegation attacks.
prompt_templates.scenario: Mid-engagement escalation during red team operation

prompt_templates.title: Domain Dominance Planning
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how to achieve domain dominance using DCSync and Golden Ticket attacks. Include the prerequisites, commands, and detection avoidance considerations for each technique.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced phase of comprehensive AD security assessment

output_examples.input: Show me Kerberoasting commands for domain example.local
output: Using Impacket from Linux: GetUserSPNs.py example.local/user:password -dc-ip 10.10.10.10 -request -outputfile hashes.txt
Cracking with hashcat: hashcat -m 13100 hashes.txt rockyou.txt

output_examples.input: How do I perform a DCSync attack?
output: DCSync requires Replicating Directory Changes rights. Using Impacket: secretsdump.py example.local/admin:password@10.10.10.10 -just-dc
This extracts all domain password hashes including krbtgt for Golden Ticket creation

best_practices: Always obtain written authorization before executing any AD attack techniques against production environments
Document all compromised accounts and accessed systems for client reporting and remediation guidance
Synchronize time with domain controller before Kerberos attacks to avoid clock skew failures
Use stealth techniques like user enumeration limits to avoid account lockouts during password spraying
Restore any modified system state (like ZeroLogon password changes) after testing completion
anti_patterns: Never execute attack commands against environments without explicit written authorization from the owner
Do not run aggressive password spraying that could lock out legitimate user accounts
Avoid leaving Golden Tickets or other persistence mechanisms without documenting them for removal
Do not modify production Active Directory objects, GPOs, or configurations without approval

faq.question: Is this skill legal to use?
faq.answer: This skill provides educational documentation for security professionals. Only use these techniques against systems you own or have explicit written authorization to test. Unauthorized access to computer systems is illegal.

faq.question: What tools do I need to execute these attacks?
faq.answer: Common tools include Impacket (Python), Mimikatz (Windows), Rubeus (Windows), BloodHound/SharpHound, CrackMapExec, and hashcat. Most are available on Kali Linux by default.

faq.question: Can this skill automatically run attacks?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides reference documentation only. It does not execute any commands automatically. Users must manually run commands in their own environment.

faq.question: What is the difference between Kerberoasting and AS-REP Roasting?
faq.answer: Kerberoasting targets service accounts with SPNs by requesting crackable TGS tickets. AS-REP Roasting targets user accounts with 'Do not require Kerberos preauthentication' enabled by requesting crackable AS-REP responses.

faq.question: How do I detect these attacks in my environment?
faq.answer: Enable advanced audit policies for Kerberos authentication, monitor for unusual TGS requests, track DCSync patterns (replication traffic), and use BloodHound defensively to identify attack paths before adversaries do.

faq.question: What is a Golden Ticket and why is it dangerous?
faq.answer: A Golden Ticket is a forged Kerberos TGT created with the krbtgt password hash. It grants persistent domain admin access that survives password changes. Detection requires monitoring for abnormal ticket lifetimes and encryption types.
